pages_preview.text_preview: Expected statistical power: Justifying and evaluating statistical power Jeremy C. Biesanz University of British Columbia library(devtools) install_github(“jbiesanz/fabs”)

Overview 2 • Options for justifying sample size. • Why standard power calculations are often biased. • Expected statistical power. library(devtools) install_github(“jbiesanz/fabs”) • What is this? How do we interpret it? • Technical details. • Bells & whistles: Composite nulls and publication bias. • Simple code in R (really!) • Revisiting power in the OSF (2015) Science study. • Coping with the answers and results.

Justifying Sample Size 3 • Sample size in a study needs to be justified. •As an author you need to make a cogent, rational, and coherent argument. •As a reader, reviewer, and consumer of research, is the argument made by the author strong and justifiable? • Sample sized based on minimal effect size of interest? • Efficient? Can we really always do this? • Using previous research and effect size estimates. • Often there are data available to make an argument. Meta-analyses, previous research, etc. How often is there really NO relevant prior research? • Effect size estimates + G*Power Incorrect Answer

Standard Statistical Power Calculations 4 Errors in Hypothesis Testing True State of Affairs H True H False o o Four components of inference H Type II Error Outcome of o 1. Sample size (n) Correct Decision Retained Hypothesis β 2. Significance criterion (α) Test Type I Error Correct Decision 3. Effect size (e.g., δ or ρ) H o 4. Statistical power (1-β) Rejected α (power=1- β) Type I Error: Rejecting a true null hypothesis. Type II Error: Retaining a false null hypothesis. Statistical Power: The probability of correctly rejecting a false null hypothesis. Let’s not even get into Type III error rates…

Why are standard power calculations often biased? 5 • Effect size estimates are statistics. • Traditional power calculations require population parameters as input. • Even if effect size estimates are distributed symmetrically and are unbiased, the resulting statistical power calculation estimate is biased! •Why??
